title: The Peace Maker
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It has a ן¿½pointן¿½ of neither sex
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But comes in guise of both,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, doubly dangerous complex,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It is a thing to loathe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A lady with her sweet, sad smile,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A gentleman on oath.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Strip off the mother-veil, and fur!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And signs of ן¿½quiet tasteן¿½.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The dead childן¿½s locket take from her
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (The dead manן¿½s gift in haste)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And wash from every evil line
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The layers of filling paste!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From ן¿½saddened eyesן¿½ the hellן¿½s own glare!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From ן¿½sweet mouthן¿½ blasphemy!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wrench out the gold-filled false teeth there
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That twice mock honesty,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And leave the evil face awry
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For married folk to see.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For foolish girl wives in despair,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For menן¿½s and childrenן¿½s sakes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Let loose the glossed and padded hair
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To writhe like scorching snakes!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And strip the barren body bare
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To show what Satan makes.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Aye! I could take her by the throat
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ More sure than hangmanן¿½s noose,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And set my teeth and set my nails,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And hate would set my thews.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And fling her to the drought-starved swine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Were all my brethren Jews.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There was the kindest man I knew,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Brave, handsome, straight and tall,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Between his loved ones and the world
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He stood, a fortress wall.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He whines, a ruined drunkard now,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And this thing did it all.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There was the girl who married me
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bore my children twain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Weן¿½ll never meet each otherן¿½s eyes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like boy and girl again.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The very childrenן¿½s love and trust
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By this foul thing was slain.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There was a girl my manhood loved,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sheן¿½d Loveן¿½s own red gold hair,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And grey eyes that were Pityן¿½s own
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And courage that was rare.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She sleeps amongst the suicides,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And this thing sent her there.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all because the town was dull
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And goodness was too tame,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And people took no interest
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In one they could not blame.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all because my life was clean
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I had won a name.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And now, for years of senseless hate
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And paltry, bitter strife,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For ן¿½reparationן¿½ come too late,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For sweetheart, mate and wife,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I tread her vile heart in the dust
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ashes of my life.
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
